text: In this video, we are going to describe laparoscopic adhesiolysis.
The video shows dense omental adhesions over the anterior abdominal wall from a previous surgery, with the uterus visibly adherent and pulled up towards the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon initiates the laparoscopic adhesiolysis using a disposable shearer, a device that simultaneously coagulates and cuts tissue.
The surgeon uses the blades of the laparoscopic disposable shearer to perform adhesiolysis, minimizing lateral thermal spread while cutting and coagulating dense omental adhesions.
The surgeon carefully pushes the omentum downward, taking care to avoid the underlying intestines visible beneath it.
The surgeon performs gentle and precise laparoscopic dissection to maintain a bloodless plane, carefully separating adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon carefully separates the uterus from dense adhesions attached to the anterior abdominal wall, taking precautions to avoid injury to the bladder.
The surgeon inserts a Foley's catheter into the bladder, ensuring its placement to prevent bladder injury during the adhesiolysis procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of bladder adhesions from the uterus, restoring the uterus's mobility. The next step involves dissecting adhesions at the level of the adnexa.
The surgeon carefully dissects dense adhesions of the omentum at the level of the adnexa, taking precautions to avoid coagulation that could damage the ovarian reserve.
As you can notice, the patient was experiencing pain abdomen and one of the reasons could be the dense adhesions which were causing stretch over the peritoneum causing the pain.
Also could be associated subclinical infections.
The surgeon completes the surgery by liberating all adhesions, restoring normal anatomical mobility and function.
